metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the intended credential helper mechanism in the plan (e.g., 'use a custom git credential helper script that reads `GIT_PAT` from the environment variable populated by .env, installed in Dockerfile at `/usr/local/bin/git-credential-rebar-env` and configured via `git config --global credential.helper rebar-env`') so the executor has no design choice to make.
findings.finding: The git credential helper mechanism is specified by name of concept ('git credential helper on origin') but the concrete implementation is left to the executor: which helper binary (store, osxkeychain, a custom script?), how it is installed in the Dockerfile, and how it is wired to the PAT value from .env are all unspecified.
findings.location: What section — 'SSM -> .env -> git credential helper on origin; never on-disk plaintext'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan where `norm_id` and `material_fingerprint` originate — e.g. 'copy `norm_id` from the rebar ticket's own field (retrieved via the store API before emission) and compute `material_fingerprint` as SHA256 of the diff content' — so the executor has no design decision to make.
findings.finding: `norm_id` and `material_fingerprint` are listed as required join-key fields in the artifact schema but their computation source — what produces them, from what input, with what algorithm or reference — is entirely absent from the plan. An executor implementing the artifact emission code cannot populate these fields without this information.
findings.location: What section — 'carrying the impact + tier-tagged sub-answers + norm_id/material_fingerprint join keys'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or explicitly add to scope) that `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` exists as a Terraform-managed SSM SecureString in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, is fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`, injected via `ReceiverConfig`, and wired into a git credential helper — none of these exist today.
findings.finding: The plan states the PAT is sourced from SSM at `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` as if this parameter already exists, and that 'The PAT push capability is already confirmed working from the reviewbot container.' Neither the SSM parameter slot `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` nor any credential-helper wiring appears in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`, `infra/terraform/user_data.sh`, or `src/rebar/review_bot/config.py`. This is an unverifiable confident assertion: the plan implicitly scopes out the SSM provisioning work for this new param by acting as if it pre-exists.
findings.location: What section — 'the RebarBotNava PAT at `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` (SSM -> .env -> git credential helper on origin)' and 'The PAT push capability is already confirmed working from the reviewbot container.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete mechanism and file(s) for the git credential helper (e.g. a `GIT_ASKPASS` script baked into `Dockerfile.reviewbot`, or a `git config credential.helper` entrypoint step) and add them to the Scope.
findings.finding: The plan's 'git credential helper on origin' mechanism has no existing implementation anywhere in the codebase (confirmed by grep for `credential.helper`, `GIT_ASKPASS`, `reviewbot-tickets-pat`, `git credential`). The plan treats this as a known pattern but it is a net-new component. The plan does not enumerate any file where this helper would live or how it integrates with the container's git config.
findings.location: Plan §What: 'git credential helper on origin; never on-disk plaintext'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and verify the exact git credential helper mechanism (e.g., `git config credential.helper '!f() { echo username=x; echo password=$REBAR_BOT_PAT; } && f'` in Dockerfile.reviewbot, or a gitconfig insteadOf rewrite) that delivers the PAT from .env to git-push without writing plaintext to disk, and confirm fetch-secrets.sh and ssm.tf are extended with the new `reviewbot-tickets-pat` leaf.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'SSM -> .env -> git credential helper on origin' as the PAT delivery mechanism, but no prior art for this wiring exists in the codebase: Dockerfile.reviewbot has no credential helper setup, docker-compose.yml has no git config for the persistent tickets store, and fetch-secrets.sh has a fixed enumerated leaf map that does not include 'reviewbot-tickets-pat'. The mechanism by which the .env variable becomes a functioning git credential helper for origin pushes is unspecified and unverified.
findings.location: What section — 'SSM -> .env -> git credential helper on origin; never on-disk plaintext'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `credential.helper` entry to the reviewbot's git config (baked into Dockerfile.reviewbot) that reads the PAT from the environment variable materialised from SSM. Update fetch-secrets.sh to include `reviewbot_tickets_pat=$(get_param reviewbot-tickets-pat)` and write `REBAR_BOT_PAT=${reviewbot_tickets_pat}` to .env. Add `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` to ssm.tf's `rebar_secret_params` list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name and verify the concrete git credential mechanism that bridges the SSM-sourced PAT env var to authenticated `git push` within the reviewbot container without writing plaintext to disk (e.g., a custom helper script, `git config credential.helper '!f(){ echo "password=${REBAR_TICKETS_PAT}"; }; f'` injected at store-init time, or a tmpfs-backed netrc), and add this as an implementation step in the plan scope.
findings.finding: The plan's PAT delivery chain 'SSM → .env → git credential helper on origin' is asserted but unverified at the flag/command level: no git credential helper is configured in Dockerfile.reviewbot, docker-compose.yml, or fetch-secrets.sh, and the plan's own constraint ('never on-disk plaintext') rules out git-credential-store (which writes ~/.git-credentials). The bridge between an env var holding the PAT and an authenticated `git push` to origin requires a concrete mechanism (e.g., a custom credential helper script, a gitconfig `url.<base>.insteadOf`, or a netrc-over-tmpfs) — none is named or verified in the plan or codebase.
findings.location: Plan > What section — 'SSM -> .env -> git credential helper on origin; never on-disk plaintext'; also AC2 ('persistent tickets store whose origin authenticates via the PAT sourced from SSM .env')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/implementation steps: 'At reviewbot tickets-store init, configure a transient credential helper via `git config credential.helper "!f(){ echo username=x; echo password=${REBAR_TICKETS_PAT}; }; f"` run against the persistent store's git repo — this keeps the PAT in memory (env), never on disk, and is torn down with the process.' Also add 'Add `reviewbot-tickets-pat` leaf to fetch-secrets.sh and map it to env var `REBAR_TICKETS_PAT` in the .env block.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope whether `norm_id`/`material_fingerprint` will be imported from `plan_review`, moved to a shared module, or re-implemented for code_review; name the resulting source files as edit targets.
findings.finding: The plan references `norm_id` and `material_fingerprint` as join keys the `code_review` artifact carries. Both symbols are defined exclusively inside the plan-review subsystem (`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py::norm_id` and `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/pass1.py::material_fingerprint`). The plan does not enumerate these source files as edit targets or specify whether these functions will be imported from plan_review, duplicated, or newly implemented for code_review.
findings.location: Plan §What: 'carrying the impact + tier-tagged sub-answers + `norm_id`/`material_fingerprint` join keys'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `norm_id` and `material_fingerprint` in the code_review artifact reuse the existing sidecar/plan-review definitions verbatim (per-finding hashes) or introduce new artifact-level variants — and if new, specify the inputs and schema so the impact-model join is well-defined.
findings.finding: The plan proposes using `norm_id` as a join key inside the code_review artifact, but `norm_id` is currently defined as a per-finding hash derived from criterion IDs + significant tokens of a finding's text (a plan-review sidecar concept). Reusing it as an artifact-level join key for a code_review record is an unverified capability assertion — the existing `norm_id` API is scoped to individual plan-review findings, not to change-scoped artifacts.
findings.location: What section — 'carrying the impact + tier-tagged sub-answers + norm_id/material_fingerprint join keys'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'tier-tagged sub-answers' in the plan: specify the tier vocabulary (enum values), the sub-answer schema shape, and how they are derived from the existing `code_review_verdict` structure (blocking/advisory/coaching findings).
findings.finding: 'tier-tagged sub-answers' is referenced as a payload field of the new code_review artifact but is never defined anywhere in the plan or codebase — no schema, no enum for what 'tier' means, and no specification of the sub-answer vocabulary for code review.
findings.location: Plan §What: 'carrying the impact + tier-tagged sub-answers + `norm_id`/`material_fingerprint` join keys'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'tier-tagged sub-answers' with a concrete field reference, e.g.: 'the verdict's `blocking[]` and `advisory[]` findings (each tagged with their Pass-3 decision tier: `block` | `advisory`), and the `coverage` object' — matching the fields already present in `code_review_verdict.schema.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `code_review` is a new `ticket_type` enum value or a non-ticket artifact; if the former, enumerate the required registration steps: extend `common.schema.json` enum, add to `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES`, add graph exemption in `_hierarchy.py`, and confirm absence from `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a `code_review` artifact type but never specifies whether it extends the closed `ticket_type` enum (currently `["bug","epic","story","task","session_log"]` in `common.schema.json`) or uses a separate non-ticket artifact mechanism — leaving the registration protocol, EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES update, and reconciler config change unspecified.
findings.location: Plan §What: 'Emit a change-scoped `code_review` artifact' / AC1: 'A `code_review` artifact type/handling exists'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define how `norm_id` and `material_fingerprint` are computed for a diff-scoped `code_review` artifact, or replace them with the correct diff-scoped join keys (e.g., the `(gerrit-change-id, revision)` composite key already named as the primary key, plus a hash of the diff content for fingerprinting).
findings.finding: `norm_id` and `material_fingerprint` are referenced as join keys on the new artifact, but both are plan-review–specific concepts with no code-review equivalent — `norm_id` is computed from a plan-review finding's text/criteria, and `material_fingerprint` is computed from a `PlanContext` (ticket description + AC text); neither has a defined computation path for a diff-scoped artifact.
findings.location: Plan §What: 'carrying the impact + tier-tagged sub-answers + `norm_id`/`material_fingerprint` join keys'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` to the Scope as an explicit edit target and specify the new `reviewbot-tickets-pat` → env-var mapping (e.g. `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT`) that it must emit.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` is not listed as an edit target in the plan's Scope, yet it is the only script that materialises SSM params into the container `.env`. Without a new `get_param reviewbot-tickets-pat` call and a corresponding `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT=…` line in the output block, the PAT never reaches the container's environment — breaking the entire SSM→.env→credential-helper chain the plan describes.
findings.location: Plan §Scope: 'an SSM materialize step for the PAT'; §What: 'SSM -> .env -> git credential helper on origin'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (Scope or implementation notes) that `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` gains a new `get_param reviewbot-tickets-pat` line (and corresponding `REBAR_TICKETS_PAT=...` line in the `.env` stanza) so the PAT materializes idempotently on every boot/redeploy.
findings.finding: The plan says the PAT flows 'SSM -> .env -> git credential helper on origin' but does not specify that `fetch-secrets.sh` is updated to fetch and emit the new parameter. The existing script has a fixed, curated map of SSM leaves → env vars; a new leaf does not appear automatically. Without an explicit update to `fetch-secrets.sh` (or a separate materialize script called by `compose-up.sh`), the PAT will not be present in `.env` at boot and `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always` git pushes will fail unauthenticated.
findings.location: Plan > What section: 'SSM -> .env -> git credential helper'; infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` as a `SecureString` placeholder in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` (alongside the other `rebar_secret_params` entries) so the slot exists before `fetch-secrets.sh` tries to read it.
findings.finding: The plan names `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` as the SSM source for the PAT, but this parameter does not exist in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` (the canonical provisioning file that declares all `/rebar/prod/*` slots). The plan does not enumerate `ssm.tf` as an edit target.
findings.location: Plan §What: 'PAT at `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` (SSM -> .env -> git credential helper on origin)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat"` to the `rebar_secret_params` list in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` with the same `ignore_changes = [value]` lifecycle block used by other secrets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` to `ssm.tf`'s `rebar_secret_params` list (or declare a separate `aws_ssm_parameter` resource for it) so the new PAT slot is Terraform-managed and visible in `terraform plan`, consistent with all other `/rebar/prod/*` secrets.
findings.finding: The new SSM parameter `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` is not specified as Terraform-managed IaC. The existing `ssm.tf` lists every other secret slot in `rebar_secret_params` and creates them via `aws_ssm_parameter.rebar_secrets`. The plan says 'Provision the reviewbot with… SSM' but does not say the parameter is added to `ssm.tf`; if created out-of-band (e.g. via `aws ssm put-parameter` by hand) it is invisible to `terraform plan`, drifts from IaC, and is silently destroyed on a `terraform destroy`.
findings.location: Plan > What section: 'Provision the reviewbot with… the RebarBotNava PAT at `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` (SSM…)'; infra/terraform/ssm.tf
findings.suggested_fix: Append `"/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat"` to the `rebar_secret_params` local in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`. Add a comment matching the existing pattern (what it is, who consumes it, how it is materialized) and note that `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` applies so an operator populate-and-overwrite won't be reverted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py` and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_fields.py` to the Scope as edit targets for the Jira-sync exclusion requirement (mirroring the session_log pattern).
findings.finding: The plan calls for `code_review` artifacts to be excluded from the Jira sync, following the `session_log` pattern. The concrete enforcement point is `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py` (`EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES` frozenset) and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_fields.py` (`_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` dict). Neither file is enumerated as an edit target in the plan's Scope.
findings.location: Plan AC1: 'excluded from Jira sync'; Plan §Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"code_review"` to `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES` in config.py and keep it absent from `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` in outbound_fields.py; update the corresponding exclusion test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a bootstrapping spec: define who initializes the persistent store (git init/clone), when (container startup entrypoint or Dockerfile step), and how re-deployment with an existing volume is handled (skip init if .git exists).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify who creates (bootstraps) the persistent tickets store and when — whether it is provisioned at deployment time (e.g., a volume pre-initialized with a git repo and the correct origin remote), lazily on first artifact write, or by a one-time operator step. Without this, the store's initial state is undefined and implementers may make inconsistent choices.
findings.location: ## Scope / 'Reviewbot infra (docker-compose.yml, Dockerfile.reviewbot, … a persistent store)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Dockerfile.reviewbot or an entrypoint script: 'If $TICKETS_STORE_PATH/.git does not exist, clone origin/tickets there; otherwise, verify origin URL matches expected and update if needed.' Document this as the CREATE step in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly scope the `rebar init` bootstrapping of the new persistent tickets store (path, which script performs it, whether compose-up.sh seeds it, and how origin is set with push credentials) — this work does not exist today and is not mentioned.
findings.finding: The plan describes provisioning 'a PERSISTENT pushable tickets store' for the reviewbot and notes `gerrit_reviewbot` volume as the target. The existing `gerrit_reviewbot` volume (`docker-compose.yml:95-96`) is documented solely as holding 'the dedup ledger + reconcile cursor' (`config.py` comments, compose comments). No rebar tickets store (`rebar init`, `.tickets-tracker`) is initialised in this volume anywhere in the codebase. The plan's description of this as an existing 'persistent store' to be merely 'wired' rather than freshly initialised is not supported by the code.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Reviewbot infra (docker-compose.yml, Dockerfile.reviewbot, an SSM materialize step for the PAT + a persistent store)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name the filesystem path and compose volume backing the persistent tickets git repo (e.g. a subdirectory of `gerrit_reviewbot`, or a new `gerrit_tickets` external volume) and add it to the Scope's edit targets for `docker-compose.yml` and `compose-up.sh`.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope says 'a persistent store' for the reviewbot tickets, but no second volume (or enlarged use of `gerrit_reviewbot`) for a *git-backed tickets repo with a pushable origin* is enumerated as a new compose/infra edit target. The existing `gerrit_reviewbot` volume (bind-mounted at `/var/gerrit/site/reviewbot`) holds only the SQLite dedup ledger and reconcile cursor — not a git repo. The plan does not specify where the tickets store git repo lives on the container filesystem or which compose/host path backs it.
findings.location: Plan §Scope: 'a persistent store'; docker-compose.yml lines 88–95 (gerrit_reviewbot volume)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability AC: at minimum, log a structured error (with change-id + revision) on every emit or push failure, and expose a counter (e.g. `code_review_artifact_push_failed_total`) so silent failures are detectable.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for the new failure points introduced by this plan (SSM fetch failure, git push failure, artifact emission failure). Operators have no way to detect that artifacts are silently not being written or pushed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — no AC line covers monitoring/alerting for the emission path

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability specification to the plan: either a new `ARTIFACT_EMIT_ERROR` structured log marker (greppable by observability.sh, paired with a CloudWatch alarm) or an explicit statement that existing `voter_errors` coverage subsumes artifact push failures.
findings.finding: No CloudWatch alarm or observability metric is specified for the new `code_review` artifact emission path. The existing monitoring covers `voter_errors` (vote-cast failures), but the new push-to-origin step introduces a distinct failure mode: PAT expiry, git push conflict, SSM fetch failure, or network error silently drops artifacts. The plan states 'No retention cap (deliberate)' but specifies no monitoring for whether artifacts are actually landing.
findings.location: Plan > Acceptance Criteria AC2/AC3; infra/terraform/monitoring_s4b.tf; infra/scripts/observability.sh

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the PAT type and minimum required scope for `reviewbot-tickets-pat` (e.g., fine-grained PAT: single-repo `navapbc/rebar`, permission `contents:write` only) in the plan's What or AC sections, matching the pattern established for `g2p-github-pat`.
findings.finding: The plan does not declare the permission scope of the new `reviewbot-tickets-pat` GitHub PAT. The boundary-crossing push credential's access class is undeclared — it could be a classic repo-level PAT with broad write (or admin) access, or a fine-grained PAT scoped to contents:write on the `tickets` ref of a single repository. An over-privileged PAT elevates blast radius if the credential leaks from the container or `.env`.
findings.location: What section: 'the RebarBotNava PAT at `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The PAT is a fine-grained GitHub PAT scoped to repository `navapbc/rebar`, permission `contents: write` (no admin, no other repos).' Mirror the existing `g2p-github-pat` documentation pattern in `infra/runbooks/g2p-ci-credentials.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how concurrent reviewbot pushes are serialized or retried (e.g., push-with-rebase loop, a queue in front of the git write, or a uniqueness constraint on the (change, revision) key) and confirm artifact writes are idempotent under re-push of the same key.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a concurrency / serialization mechanism for the case where multiple parallel review jobs push to the same persistent tickets store simultaneously. With REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always and no mention of a queue, lock, or retry-with-rebase strategy, concurrent pushes to origin/tickets will produce push rejections or require force-pushes, risking lost writes or corrupted history.
findings.location: ## What / 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always' and ## Scope / 'Reviewbot infra'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a push strategy note: 'On push conflict, rebase the local commit onto origin/tickets and retry (up to N times); the (gerrit-change-id, revision) key makes the write idempotent — a duplicate write is a no-op or overwrites identically.' This covers both the race and the re-review case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs (or explicit plan text) covering at least: (a) behavior when the PAT/SSM credential is missing or invalid at startup (fail-loud or degrade gracefully with a logged error?), (b) behavior when the push to origin/tickets is rejected (retry? log-and-continue?), and (c) behavior on a malformed/missing gerrit-change-id trailer.
findings.finding: The new emission path (PAT push + artifact write) has happy-path-only failure coverage in the ACs. Failure modes for credential unavailability (SSM down at startup, expired/revoked PAT), push rejection, and malformed change-id input are not addressed, leaving significant operational failure paths untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2 and AC3; What section ('never on-disk plaintext', 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback protocol for artifact emit failure (store write or push): specify whether the review gate proceeds (best-effort, log-and-continue) or is held, and whether a greppable error marker is emitted to structured logs.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no fallback or error-handling protocol for failure of the artifact emission sub-step itself (write to persistent store + push to origin), beyond the AC3 'unresolved trailer refs are logged, not fatal' rule which covers only the relates_to linking sub-step.
findings.location: AC3: 'unresolved/foreign trailer refs are logged, not fatal' / Plan §What: 'wire the emission path'
findings.suggested_fix: Model the emit on the existing code_review/sidecar.py pattern: best-effort, log a greppable ARTIFACT_EMIT_FAILED marker with (change_id, revision) on any exception, never raise into the gate — and note this explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit requirement (in the What section or AC2) that git push stderr on the new PAT-authenticated emission path must be redacted (token + URL-encoded token) before logging, mirroring the existing `gerrit_client.py` redaction pattern.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'SSM → .env → git credential helper on origin' for the PAT but does not require log-redaction of the PAT on the git push error path. The existing bot-token push path in `gerrit_client.py` (lines 350–352) explicitly redacts the token from git stderr before logging; the new artifact-emission push path carries no analogous redaction requirement. If git echoes an authenticated URL in an error message (e.g., `fatal: repository 'https://<token>@github.com/...' not found`), the PAT lands in journald logs unredacted.
findings.location: What section / AC2: 'SSM -> .env -> git credential helper on origin; never on-disk plaintext'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'The git push error handler for the emission path MUST redact the PAT and its URL-encoded form from git stderr before emitting any log line, matching the `gerrit_client.py` pattern (lines 347–353).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `code_review` emit path is idempotent on (gerrit-change-id, revision): define whether the writer does an upsert (overwrite existing artifact file for that key) or a 'skip-if-exists' check before committing, so retries after push failure cannot produce duplicate store entries.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify upsert/idempotent write semantics for the `code_review` artifact when the emit is retried after a partial failure (e.g., local commit succeeded but `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always` push to origin failed). Re-running the emit could produce a duplicate artifact for the same (gerrit-change-id, revision) key, violating the implicit uniqueness implied by that keying scheme.
findings.location: AC line 3 / 'What' section — emit call + REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always
findings.suggested_fix: Before committing the artifact, check whether a file keyed by `{gerrit_change_id}-{revision}.json` already exists in the store; if it does and content is identical, skip the commit (no-op push). If content differs (e.g., re-review), overwrite and amend/force-push or append with a new revision suffix. Document the chosen semantics in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a scope line committing to a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md entry covering: the session_log pattern contract (dependency-graph exclusion, Jira-sync exclusion, searchable), the PAT credential chain rationale, and the code_review artifact key/relates_to semantics.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — the reviewbot gaining a persistent tickets store, a PAT credential chain, and an emission pipeline — but does not commit to capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design doc. Future maintainers have no durable record of why this pattern was chosen over alternatives.
findings.location: Scope section and What section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'Why' premise: the clone already has an `origin` remote pointing at the GitHub mirror for read-only ticket access (`gerrit_client.py:321-343`); the actual gap is that no writable/push-credentialed tickets store exists, and clarify whether the new persistent store shares or replaces the per-clone ephemeral origin.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the reviewbot only has ephemeral gate snapshots under /tmp with no origin remote' as the motivation premise. The live code in `gerrit_client.py:321-343` already adds an `origin` remote (pointing at `self._cfg.tickets_remote`, the GitHub mirror) and fetches `refs/heads/tickets` into every change clone. The plan's framing that 'no origin remote' exists is empirically refuted — what is actually absent is a WRITABLE tickets store with push credentials, which is a different and narrower gap.
findings.location: Why section — 'the reviewbot only has ephemeral gate snapshots under /tmp with no origin remote'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'no origin remote' with 'the per-review clone's origin is read-only (public HTTPS, no push credential) and ephemeral (temp dir per review)' to accurately name the gap and avoid implementation confusion about what already exists vs. what is new.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC — e.g. for AC1: `rebar artifact show --type code_review --key '<change-id>:<rev>' | jq -e '.relates_to | length > 0'`; for AC2: a docker-compose exec that pushes a dummy commit via the PAT and confirms `origin/tickets` advances; for AC3: a post-review `git log origin/tickets -1 --format=%s` check naming the artifact; for AC4: `grep -rn 'retention' config/ | wc -l` asserting 0.
findings.finding: No proving command exists for any of the four acceptance criteria. The plan states what 'done looks like' but never specifies a concrete command or structured check that would produce evidence. An executor completing this work has no specified way to verify correctness before signing off.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence in the What/Scope body describing where and how unresolved/foreign trailer refs are logged (e.g., the emit function logs a warning at WARN level to the reviewbot's stderr/structured log before continuing), so AC3's error-handling clause has body coverage and can be verified.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies a concrete error-handling behavior ('unresolved/foreign trailer refs are logged, not fatal') that has no corresponding description in the plan body (What or Scope sections), leaving the implementer without guidance on HOW this logging is wired or WHERE it surfaces.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3 / What section
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, under the emission-path description, add: 'If a commit trailer references a ticket ID that cannot be resolved in the local store or is otherwise foreign, the emit function logs a structured warning (e.g., WARN unresolved_trailer ref=<id>) and continues — the artifact is still written.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a brief note in the What or Scope section acknowledging the no-retention-cap decision and its rationale (e.g., 'artifacts accumulate indefinitely; pruning is deferred until corpus size warrants it'), or remove AC4 if it is purely informational and not a verifiable criterion.
findings.finding: AC4 ('No retention cap (deliberate)') is an orphan acceptance criterion: the plan body (Why, What, Scope) never describes this decision, provides rationale for it, or identifies what storage growth behaviour the deliberate absence of a cap implies.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the SSM fetch (retry with backoff, or fail-fast with clear error) and for the git push (retry policy, timeout, and whether a push failure is non-fatal to the review gate — log-and-continue vs hard-fail).
findings.finding: The plan adds external failure points (SSM parameter fetch, git push to origin) with no error handling, retry, backoff, or graceful-degradation strategy specified for either. If SSM fetch fails at startup the credential is absent; if the git push fails mid-review the artifact is silently lost or the review gate hangs — no fallback or degraded mode is described.
findings.location: What section — 'SSM -> .env -> git credential helper on origin' and 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a startup probe that retries SSM fetch up to N times with exponential backoff and logs a clear fatal error if exhausted. For the push, treat push failure as non-fatal to the review gate (log error, emit a metric/alert) so a transient origin outage does not block code reviews; the locally-persisted artifact remains durable for a later retry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC that validates the corpus is consumable for its stated purpose — e.g. 'a sample query/join on (norm_id, material_fingerprint) against the written artifacts returns the expected review record' or 'the impact model pipeline can ingest artifacts written by this change in a smoke run.'
findings.finding: No AC carries a concrete validation mechanism that confirms the corpus is actually usable for its stated purpose (calibrating the impact model). All four ACs are system-internal checks; none represent an observable improvement to the named beneficiary or a measurable outcome.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four items)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `src/rebar/review_bot/voter.py` (and/or `adapter.py`) as explicit edit targets in the Scope, and specify the precise call-site where the emit call is wired (e.g. after `store.record_vote` in `review_and_vote`, within the existing lock).
findings.finding: `src/rebar/review_bot/voter.py` and `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` are the live callers of the review path and the natural insertion points for the emit call. They are not enumerated as edit targets in the plan's Scope, yet the emission of the `code_review` artifact must happen at or after the verdict is produced in `voter.py` (post-`produce_code_review_verdict`) or in `adapter.py`. These are unenumerated consumers the change requires updating.
findings.location: Plan §Scope: 'the emission code (`code_review` artifact type/handling + the emit call)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (Scope or AC2) exactly where the persistent tickets store resides (e.g. inside the existing `gerrit_reviewbot` EBS bind-mount, or a new named external volume), and confirm it is covered by `external: true` in `docker-compose.yml` (so `docker compose down -v` cannot destroy it) and by the existing DLM backup.
findings.finding: The persistent reviewbot tickets store (the new git repo/worktree that `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always` pushes to) has no specified lifecycle protection. The plan says 'a persistent store' but does not state where it lives (inside `gerrit_reviewbot` volume? a new named volume? a new directory on the EBS host?), whether it is backed up by DLM, or whether `prevent_destroy` / `external: true` applies. If it lives inside the container's ephemeral filesystem rather than on a named/external volume, a `docker compose down` or container replace destroys the entire local artifact history.
findings.location: Plan > What section: 'Provision the reviewbot with a PERSISTENT pushable tickets store'; Plan > Scope: 'a persistent store'; docker-compose.yml gerrit_reviewbot volume

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback store-state assertion: explicitly document that already-pushed `code_review` artifacts are intentionally retained on code rollback (compensating-forward), or specify a pruning/tombstone mechanism, so the store state after rollback is defined rather than implicit.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the state of the persistent tickets store after a code rollback of this feature. AC[4] declares 'No retention cap (deliberate)', but the plan is silent on what happens to already-written `code_review` artifacts if the feature is reverted — the store retains orphaned artifacts with no schema rollback path described.
findings.location: AC line 4 — 'No retention cap (deliberate)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan how the persistent tickets store's origin URL differs from the read-only `TICKETS_REMOTE` public URL (e.g., use a credential helper rather than embedding the PAT in the URL) and specify whether a new env var or an `insteadOf` gitconfig rewrite is used, to avoid both the 403-push failure and the on-disk-plaintext violation.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the naming collision between the existing read-only `origin` remote (pointing at the public GitHub mirror, added by `gerrit_client.py:322` on every per-change clone) and the new persistent tickets store that must also use `origin` for push. The plan says to wire 'the PAT … on origin' but `ReceiverConfig.tickets_remote` (the public HTTPS URL, no auth) is what the current code sets as `origin` — a PAT push requires either a different remote name, a PAT-embedded URL, or an `insteadOf` URL rewrite, none of which is specified.
findings.location: Plan > What section — 'persistent pushable tickets store … origin; never on-disk plaintext'; src/rebar/review_bot/gerrit_client.py:322 (existing `remote add origin`)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
